segments.s543-g81: ببالید is a past/inchoative intransitive verb meaning 'became proud / rose (in pride)'; here it comes first and sets the action. بر سان is a comparative prepositional phrase ('بر' + 'سان' = 'like/as'), so بر (preposition) + سان ('like') introduce the simile. سرو سهی is the simile's noun + epithet: سرو (cypress) plus سهی, a poetic epithet meaning 'dark/tawny'; the phrase literally means 'like a dark cypress.' The verb governs the following noun phrase which supplies the comparison.
segments.s543-g82: خردمند is the subject/noun phrase 'the wise (man)' that links back to the verb; Persian often places the subject after the verb in poetic register. با ... و با ... are two coordinated prepositional phrases ('with beauty and with nobility') where زیب is a noun 'beauty/ornament' and فرهی is a poetic noun/adjective meaning 'nobility, dignity, culturedness.' There is no copula here; these 'با' phrases describe how the wise one appeared.
segments.s543-g83: مر here functions as a dative/emphatic 'to me' (an archaic/poetic clitic), followed by او را where را is the definite/object marker stressing the person. دران بوم is a locative phrase 'in that land/realm.' همتا نبود is the negated past copula 'there was no equal.' The whole clause thus reads literally 'To me, her in that land a match there was not.'
segments.s543-g84: به کشور is a prepositional phrase 'for/in the country/realm' introducing the domain. چنو is a comparative 'such/as' modifying the following noun phrase سرو بالا ('tall/lofty cypress' with بالا as an adjective after the noun in poetic word order). نبود is again the past negative 'was not'—together: 'in the realm there was no such tall cypress.' This repeats/emphasizes the lack of an equal.
segments.s543-g33: وگر نیست = a conditional 'if/but if (it) is not'; این = 'this' (the thing just mentioned); تا introduces a subordinate result/clause 'that...' and نباشم is first-person negative subjunctive/present ('that I should not be' / 'that I were not'); به رنج = a short prepositional phrase 'in/with hardship'. Together the clause means roughly 'and if this were not (the case), that I be in hardship...'.
segments.s543-g34: برین گونه = 'in this manner/way' (بر + این/ین forming an adverbial); نپراگند is a negative past/passive verb 'did not scatter' or 'was not scattered'; نیز گنج = 'also the treasure'. The clause states the consequence: 'the treasure would not have been scattered in this way.'
segments.s543-g35: بیامد is past tense 'came' and is placed before its subject (typical poetic order); فرستاده شهریار = 'the king's messenger' (فرستاده modified by شهریار).
segments.s543-g36: بر کید = 'on/under a stratagem' or 'as part of a plot' (بر + کید); با هدیه و با نثار repeats با 'with' for emphasis: 'with a gift and with an offering/tribute'. The phrase describes the envoy's pretence or means.
segments.s543-g37: بگفت = 'he said' (narrative past); آنک = relative 'that which/what'; با او شهنشاه گفت is a subordinate clause 'the king said to him' (با او = 'to him', شهنشاه = subject, گفت = the king's speech/words). The whole means 'he said what the king told him.'
segments.s543-g38: همه رازها = 'all the secrets' (object); برگشاد is a compound past/participle 'were opened/revealed'; از نهفت = 'from hiding/from concealment' (از + نهفت). The clause states that every secret was unveiled from concealment.
segments.s543-g39: بپرسید = 'he asked' (past); زو = poetic contraction of 'از او' meaning 'of/from him' or 'to him' in the sense 'asked him about'; کید = 'the plot' is the object; و غمخواره شد = coordinate clause 'and became grief-stricken' with شد as the copula/auxiliary 'became'.
segments.s543-g40: ز پرسش = 'from/through questioning' (ز = from, پرسش = question); سوی دانش و چاره شد = 'he turned/tended toward knowledge and a solution' (سوی = toward, دانش = learning, چاره = remedy), with شد marking the resulting change of state.
segments.s543-g41: بیاورد is a past tense verb 'he brought' placed before its object (typical poetic inversion). صلاب is the direct object of بیاورد. The coordinating وَ connects the two actions: after bringing صلاب the subject also performs the next verb گرفت, where اختر is the object of گرفت ('he seized/took a star'). Both verbs are simple past, describing successive actions.
segments.s543-g42: یکی before a noun is the indefinite determiner 'one / a certain' modifying زیج (an astronomical table). رومی is a postnominal adjective/nisba specifying the origin of the zij. به بر در + گرفت is an idiomatic, compound directional phrase with the verb گرفت ('he took'); together they indicate taking the zij into his possession (literally toward/on/in), so the prepositions/distribution words are part of a locative/possessive construction.
segments.s543-g43: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه (a look/gaze) + کرد (did) = 'he looked' or 'he fixed his gaze'. بر introduces the object/topic, so بر کار چرخ بلند is a prepositional phrase 'upon the work/affairs of the high/lofty wheel (i.e., the heavens)'. Adjective بلند follows its noun, a common Persian order.
segments.s543-g44: ز is the poetic preposition meaning 'concerning/for' (literally 'from') and introduces a coordinated list of considerations. The conjunction و simply links the nouns: 'آسانی و سود و درد و گزند' = 'ease and benefit and suffering and harm.' The string names both favourable and adverse outcomes considered by the observer.
segments.s543-g45: فرستاده را uses the object marker را to mark 'the messenger' as the definite object. گفت کردم is a periphrastic reporting of speech — گفت (said) + کردم (I did) yields 'I said' / 'I told'. شمار is the direct object here and means 'the count/tally' that he reports. Word order is object (فرستاده را) + verb phrase + object (شمار) because of poetic compression.
segments.s543-g46: این segment is two parallel prepositional phrases introduced by از 'from/of': از ایران and از اختر شهریار. The repeated از gives equal grammatical weight to both items: 'from Iran and from the (star of the) king/sovereign.' اختر شهریار is a noun-noun sequence where اختر ('star') is closely associated with شهریار ('the sovereign') — contextually 'the sovereign's star' or the stellar sign of the king.
segments.s543-g47: گر is the conditional particle 'if'. از introduces the source phrase 'from the گوهر (essence/gem) of مهرک (Mehrak)', where مهرک is a proper name. نوش‌زاد is a compound adjective/participle meaning 'newborn' or 'fresh-born' and modifies an implied human/offspring; the clause is the protasis of the conditional.
segments.s543-g48: برآمیزد is a third-person singular subjunctive/optative form ('may/might mingle, be joined') of a verb meaning 'to mix or be joined'. این تخمه is 'this seed/offspring' (تخمه = offspring/seed) and با آن نژاد 'with that lineage' completes the clause: the subjunctive verb expresses the hoped-for or potential joining of one line with another.
segments.s543-g57: گر introduces a conditional ('if'). او is the subject 'he'. زین is the noun 'this saddle' (object of the verb). نپیچد is the negative present/subjunctive/optative form of پیچیدن ('does not turn/ride away / does not unfasten'); in poetry the verb can appear before or after its logical subject. سپهر بلند is a nominal phrase 'lofty/heavenly sky' that will act in the main clause that follows.
segments.s543-g58: کند is the 3rd-person singular present/future of کردن used as the main verb of the clause ('will make / will bring about'). اینک is a discourse adverb 'now' (or 'behold') marking the speaker's following words. گفتم is the past 'I said' (here introducing direct speech). برو is the imperative 'go', and ارجمند is an honorific/adjective addressing the addressee ('esteemed' / 'noble one').
segments.s543-g59: فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger'. آمد is the past 'came'. بر plus a following noun (بر شهریار) is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/toward the king' — in older/poetic Persian بر can mark motion toward.
segments.s543-g60: بگفت is the past reporting verb 'he said' / 'told'. آنچ is a relative 'that which' (object). بشنید here is past 'he had heard'. زان is a prepositional/ pronominal head 'from that / from him', and نامدار modifies that referent 'the renowned/celebrated one' from whom the news came.
segments.s543-g61: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'when'. بشنید is past 'he heard'. گفتار او is the phrase 'his speech' (گفتار = speech, او = his), and اردشیر placed at the end is the noun 'Ardashir' who performs the action (poetic word order with the subject after the clause).
segments.s543-g62: دلش combines دل 'heart' with the possessive suffix ش 'his' = 'his heart'. گشت is the past of گشتن/شدن 'became'. پر before a noun is the adjective 'full (of)' and takes درد 'sorrow' as its complement. و is the conjunction 'and'. رخ 'face' is linked by the simile چـون 'like/as' to the proper name زریر (Zarir), so 'his face was like Zarir's.'
segments.s543-g63: فرستاده را uses را to mark the indirect object 'to the messenger' (addressed party). گفت here is the past reporting verb 'he said' and is immediately followed by the formulaic optative هرگز مباد meaning 'may it never be / God forbid'.
segments.s543-g64: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that / that it be the case that'). من is the subject 'I'. بینم is the present/subjunctive of 'to see' used here in the optative sense ('that I see'). از introduces the origin/partitive phrase 'of/from the seed (تخم)'. تخم مهرک نژاد is a compact expression meaning 'the stock/seed of Mehrak's lineage' (تخم = seed/origin, مهرک = the name Mehrak, نژاد = race/lineage).
segments.s543-g9: همی گفت is a narrative past with the iterative particle همی (here giving a continuous/habitual or emphatic flavor) plus گفت 'said'. کز introduces a content clause ('that' / 'of which') and here functions together with the following phrase as the source/target of the asking: 'from/of the Maker of the world.' Word order is poetic: the clause introducer + source precede the verb of the subordinate clause.
segments.s543-g10: بخواهم is first-person present/subjunctive (optative) 'I will / I wish to ask' used inside reported speech — English renders it as 'he would ask' or 'he would seek'. The following همی again adds an iterative/emphatic coloring. آشکار و نهان are paired nouns/adjectives 'the manifest (clear)' and 'the hidden' and serve as the object/theme of بخواهم: 'to ask about what is manifest and hidden.'
segments.s543-g11: که begins a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('that' / 'so that'). بی‌دشمن is a prepositional/adjectival phrase 'without enemies' modifying how he would possess the world. آرم is a first‑person optative/present form ('may I have' / 'let me have') in elevated/poetic usage; جهان را is the definite direct object (را marks it), and به دست is a locative/instrumental phrase 'in (my) hand' = 'in my power/control.' The poetic word order places modifiers before the verb.
segments.s543-g12: نباشم is a negative subjunctive/optative 'may I not be' (used idiomatically to mean 'I will be nothing other than...'). مگر here means 'except' (limiting the predicate). شاد و یزدان‌پرست are predicates joined by و: 'joyful and God‑worshipping / pious.' The whole line therefore says he will be none but joyful and pious when he holds the world without enemies.
segments.s543-g13: بدو is the prepositional clitic meaning 'to him' (dative). گفت is the past narrative 'said.' فرخنده is an epithet or name 'the auspicious one' (subject/speaker). دستور اوی is 'his command/instruction' with اوی an archaic third‑person possessive 'his/her/its' — the phrase means 'he gave him his instruction.'
segments.s543-g14: که here introduces the content of what was said (like 'which was:'). The sequence ای (vocative 'O') + شاه addresses the king directly: 'O king.' روشن‌دل and راه‌جوی are epithets 'bright‑hearted' and 'path‑seeking / seeker (of the right way),' joined by و. In older Persian the introducer and vocative may appear fused or run together in text.
segments.s543-g15: سوی is a directional preposition 'toward / to.' کید هندی is a compound noun 'Indian plot/stratagem' (kayd = intrigue, هندی specifying 'Indian' — likely referring to an adversary or scheme). فرستیم is first‑person plural present/subjunctive or hortative 'let us send' / 'we should send' (a directive reported in speech). کس is an indefinite 'someone' and is the direct object of فرستیم.
segments.s543-g16: که introduces a relative clause modifying کس: 'who/that.' دانش پژوهست is a fused classical form meaning 'is a seeker of knowledge' or 'learned/seeking learning' (دانش 'learning' + پژوهَست 'is seeking/one who seeks'). و joins the next predicate; فریادرس is a compound noun 'one who brings help/rescue' (a succorer). Together they specify the desirable qualifications of the person to be sent.
segments.s543-g1: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. شاپور is the personal name (here the Sasanian king). شد is the past of شدن 'to become' and takes the predicate following it (همچو سرو بلند). The adjective phrase همچو سرو بلند ('like a tall cypress') follows the verb in the poemically inverted order: literally 'when Shapur became like a tall cypress.'
segments.s543-g2: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'. چشم بدش combines چشم 'gaze/eye' + بد 'evil' to form 'evil eye', with the pronominal suffix ش 'his' attached to بد (written after چشم); بود is the past copula 'there was/was', and بیم گزند ('fear of harm') is the nominal complement. The clause therefore means 'there was fear of harm from his evil eye' → 'he feared harm from the evil eye.'
segments.s543-g3: نبودی is the past form 'you were' (here negative: 'you were not'); جدا is an adjective 'apart' placed after the verb in poetic inversion; یک زمان means 'for a while/for a time'; ز اردشیر = 'from Ardashir' (i.e. 'apart from Ardashir'). The literal order is inverted for poetic effect: 'you were not apart for a time from Ardashir.'
segments.s543-g4: ورا is the dative/locative 'to him' (referring back to Ardashir). همچو is 'like/as'. دستور here functions as a role or guiding function ('dastur' = director/guide/ordinance) modifying the following nominal predicate. بودی is the past 'you were', and وزیر is 'vizier' (the predicate). Together: 'to him, like a director, you were vizier.'
segments.s543-g5: نپرداختی is a negated past verb 'you did not (pay/attend/manage)' used here in the sense 'you did not spare / you did not keep away'. شاه is 'the king' (object/affected person), روزی 'a day' (here 'for a single day'), ز + جنگ = 'from battle/war'. The clause says roughly 'you did not keep the king away from war for even one day.'
segments.s543-g6: به شادی = 'to/for joy' (i.e. 'in rejoicing'). نبودیش is the nominal 'his absence' (نبود + pronominal suffix -یش). جای درنگ literally 'place of delay' idiomatically means 'no reason/place for delay'—here negated by context—so the whole clause means 'there was no need to delay in rejoicing his absence.'
segments.s543-g7: چو = 'when'. جایی = 'in some place/wherever'. ز دشمن = 'from/against the enemy' (here 'encountering the enemy'). بپرداختی is the past 2nd-person form 'you dealt with/attended to' (poetically used to mean 'you engaged/handled/defeated'). The clause: 'when at some place you engaged the enemy.'
segments.s543-g8: دگر here means 'again/once more'. بدکنش 'the ill‑doer/evildoer' is a noun phrase; سر 'head' is the direct object (the ezāfe linking is omitted in verse: بدکنشِ سر). برافراختی is the 2nd-person past 'you raised/erected'. Together: 'again you raised the head of the evildoer.'
segments.s543-g25: فرستاد is a 3rd-person past tense verb 'he sent' (verb first, typical of older prose/poetry). نزدیک modifies دانا and here functions adjectivally ('the sage who was near / nearby'); به هند is a prepositional phrase marking destination ('to India'). The whole chunk therefore gives the sender's action and the intended recipient/place: 'he sent (to) the nearby sage, to India'.
segments.s543-g26: بسی is a quantifier modifying the following items (classical/poetic form meaning 'many'/'much'); a list of objects follows in simple coordination (اسپ و دینار و چندی پرند) — 'horses and dinars and some birds.' Note classical register allows singular forms to represent a collection. Conjunction و links the nouns.
segments.s543-g27: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said'). رو is an imperative/directional 'go' or 'turn' (classical usage equivalent to برو), پیش دانا = 'in the presence of / before the sage' (پیش marks the addressee/location), and بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive form 'say' (second-person instruction). Together: 'He said to him: go and say before the sage.'
segments.s543-g28: که‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory formula (classical 'که ای' = 'O'), introducing direct speech addressed to someone. مرد is 'man' (the addressee), نیک‌اختر is an epithet or proper-name-like epithet ('Nīk‑akhtar' = 'fortunate‑star' / a laudatory title), and راه‌جوی is a compound meaning 'seeker/way-seeker' (one who seeks the road/path). Word order places the vocative first followed by nominal epithets.
segments.s543-g29: به اختر نگه کن is an imperative: به + اسم marks the object/target ('over Akhtar'), نگه کن = 'keep watch / look after' (نگه = 'watch/keeping', کن = imperative 'do'). که introduces the subordinate clause; تا = 'until' marks the temporal limit, من = subject 'I', and ز جنگ = 'from war' (ز is the poetic form of 'from').
segments.s543-g30: کی آسایم here functions as the verbal part of the 'until' clause: کی (classical/interrogative particle often meaning 'when' or used rhetorically) with آسایم (1st‑person present/subjunctive/optative of 'to be at rest') yields the sense 'until I am at rest / relieved from war'. و connects the next coordinated verb clause: کشور آرم به چنگ = 'I bring the country into (my) grasp' (آرم = 'I bring/secure', به چنگ = 'into the hand/grasp' — idiom for seizing/conquering). The sequence combines two goals limited by تا.
segments.s543-g31: اگر introduces a condition ('if'). بود is a form of 'to be' used here in the conditional/optative clause, and خواهد is a modal/future/volitional verb ('wishes/ will') — together they express 'if (there) is a desire/will'. بدین دستگاه is an archaic/prepositional phrase: ب + دین (classical form of 'this') + دستگاه ('system/arrangement'), meaning 'for/by/with this system' or 'this arrangement.' The clause therefore reads 'if (there) is a will for this arrangement.'
segments.s543-g32: به تدبیر آن زور is an instrumental phrase: به + تدبیر ('by/with the counsel/plan') + آن ('that') + زور ('strength/might') — 'by the counsel of that strength'. بنمای is an imperative ('show!' / 'make manifest!') in the classical poetic form, and راه is the object 'the way'/'the path.' Together: 'By that strength's counsel, show the way.'
segments.s543-g73: چو آگاه/temporal conditional: 'چو' here = 'when/if'. The phrase is literal: 'بر آتش' (preposition + noun) = 'upon/on the fire', followed by the verb clause 'یابمش بریان کنم' where 'یابمش' is the present/past narrative ('find him/it') and 'بریان کنم' is a verb phrase 'I will roast (him)' with 'کنم' as the 1st person singular present/subjunctive auxiliary ('I make/do'), giving a strong volitional threat. Word order is poetic: prepositional phrase + temporal particle + verb cluster; the object of 'بریان کنم' is implied (the one found).
segments.s543-g74: This is a coordinated result/threat clause. 'برو' here functions as a simple verb ('go' / 'I will make [it so]') introducing the consequence phrase; 'خاک را' uses 'را' as the definite/object marker marking 'the earth' as the affected thing; 'زار و گریان کنم' are two predicative adjectives/participles ('wretched' and 'weeping') linked by 'و', with final 'کنم' again the 1st person verb 'I make/do' — together: 'I (will) make the earth wretched and weeping.' The sequence is poetic inversion but grammatically: verb (برو) + object (خاک را) + complements (زار و گریان) + auxiliary (کنم).
segments.s543-g75: 'به جهرم فرستاد چندی سوار' = 'to Jahrom he sent several riders.' 'به' is the preposition 'to'; 'جهرم' is the place name; 'فرستاد' is the simple past 'he sent' (verb final in prose, but here placed before its noun phrase by poetic inversion); 'چندی' modifies 'سوار' meaning 'several' (not 'for a while' here). The sequence shows classical/poetic flexibility in ordering the verb and its quantified object.
segments.s543-g76: This nominative apposition identifies one of the riders: 'یکی مرد جوینده و کینه‌دار' = 'one (a certain) man, a seeker and resentful.' 'یکی' is indefinite 'one/a certain'; 'مرد' is the noun 'man'; 'جوینده' is a participial noun 'seeker' describing him; 'و' joins the second descriptive adjective 'کینه‌دار' ('resentful/harboring hatred'). The copula 'is' is omitted (typical of Persian).
segments.s543-g77: 'چو آگاه شد دخت مهرک بجست' is a temporal clause plus main action: 'چو آگاه شد' = 'when she became aware' ('چو' = when; 'آگاه شد' = became aware'), then the subject 'دخت مهرک' (the maiden Mehrak) followed by 'بجست' (past tense 'leapt/sprang'). The auxiliary 'شد' forms a past-state ('became aware'), and the sudden action 'بجست' follows; this order (temporal clause → subject → verb) is normal in older Persian narrative.
segments.s543-g78: 'سوی خان مهتر به کنجی نشست' describes direction and location: 'سوی' = 'toward/in the presence of', 'خان مهتر' = the title 'khan Mahter' or 'chief khan' (compound noun), then 'به کنجی نشست' = 'sat into/at a corner' ('به' = to/toward; 'کنجی' = nook/corner; 'نشست' = sat down).' Word order places the directional phrase before the verb, a standard pattern in classical Persian.
segments.s543-g79: 'چو بنشست آن دخت مهرک بده' begins with the temporal 'چو بنشست' = 'when she sat down.' 'آن دخت مهرک' is the subject 'that maiden Mehrak.' The verb 'بده' here is narrative (classical/historic present or reported present) meaning 'gives' or 'gave' in the storytelling register; the direct object is omitted in the line and supplied by the following clause, so this clause sets up an action she performs upon sitting.
segments.s543-g80: 'مر او را گرامی همی کرد مه' explains to whom and how: 'مر' is a dative/clitic pronoun 'to me', placed before the object; 'او را' is the direct object 'him' (marked with 'را'); 'گرامی' is a predicate/adjective 'dear/esteemed'; 'همی کرد' is the past/narrative compound ('همی' = continuous/habitual marker in classical narration + 'کرد' = 'did/made') meaning 'she made/treated (him) as esteemed'; final 'مه' is an intensifier 'great/very.' The literal sense: 'she made him esteemed to me, very [much].'
segments.s543-g49: نشیند is a third‑person subjunctive/optative form meaning roughly “may he sit” or, in a conditional sense, “if he sits”; Persian often places the verb before its complements. به آرام is an adverbial phrase (به used adverbially = “in/with”) meaning “calmly/peacefully.” بر تخت شاه is the prepositional phrase “on the king's throne” (بر = on/upon, تخت = throne, شاه = king); the subject is omitted and understood from the verb.
segments.s543-g50: نباید is an impersonal modal meaning “one should not / it must not”; it governs the following verb. فرستاد here functions as the verb “send/dispatch” (bare infinitive/verb form used after نباید). هر سو سپاه is the phrase ‘troops to every quarter’ (هر سو = every direction/each side, سپاه = army/troops); word order places the direction phrase before the noun it qualifies.
segments.s543-g51: بیفزایدش is a third‑person present/subjunctive form with the enclitic -ش (‘him/it’) meaning ‘adds to it / increases it’. The verb is followed by its object گنج (treasure). و links the clauses. کاهدش is parallel to بیفزایدش and (here) means ‘reduces/lessens it’, followed by رنج ‘hardship/suffering’; the enclitic -ش again refers back to the same object/agent. The clause states two opposing effects (more treasure, less toil).
segments.s543-g52: تو شو is an imperative sequence: تو (you) plus شو (become) meaning ‘you become’ or ‘take on’. کینه is the noun ‘hatred/grudge’ functioning as the predicate or the object of the imperative (‘become hatred / harbour hatred’). این دو گوهر بسنج is a second imperative: این دو (these two) modifies گوهر (gems/essences), and بسنج is the 2nd‑singular imperative ‘weigh/consider’ — instructing the addressee to weigh/compare the two.
segments.s543-g53: گر is the conditional 'if'. این کرد is a short clause 'does this / has done this' (کرد = did/made). ایران ورا گشت literally 'Iran turned to him' (ورا = to him/thither), meaning 'Iran became his'; گشت راست adds ‘became true/right’ or ‘became secure/established’ — together indicating that, if he acts so, Iran will fall to him or be firmly his.
segments.s543-g54: بیابد is a verb meaning 'he finds/attains' (here: he will attain/obtain). همه کام دل is the object phrase 'all the desires of the heart' (همه = all, کام = desire/wish, دل = heart). هرچ خواست is a relative/qualifying phrase 'whatever he desired' (هرچ = whatever, خواست = desire/will) — together: 'he will obtain every wish of the heart.'
segments.s543-g55: فرستاده را uses را as the dative/definite object marker here ('to the messenger'). چیز is an indefinite object 'a thing/something.' بخشید is the past tense 'gave/bestowed' (subject omitted but understood). و گفت links the action to speech — 'and said' — introducing the reported clause that follows.
segments.s543-g56: کزین is a prepositional compound 'from this / of this' introducing the material of speech. هرچ گفتم means 'whatever I said' (هرچ = whatever, گفتم = I said); the speaker reports that nothing of that should be concealed. نباید نهفت is an impersonal negative construction: نباید 'must not / should not' plus نهفت (past participle/infinitive form 'to be hidden' / 'be hidden') — so: 'should not be hidden' or 'must not be kept secret.'
segments.s543-g65: This phrase is a compact, somewhat inverted Persian clause. به خانه درون = 'into/into the house' (به + خانه + درون, prepositional phrase with درون postposed for emphasis). آرم is a 1st-person present/optative form meaning 'I rest / I shelter' (poetic usage). ز کوی = 'from the quarter/neighborhood' (ز = 'from', کوی = 'quarter'); word order is poetic, so the prepositional phrases surround the verb.
segments.s543-g66: شود is the impersonal/auxiliary 'becomes' or 'will become'. با + بر و بوم is a prepositional phrase 'with (my) land/realm' where بر و بوم is a fixed paired expression meaning 'land, dwelling, homeland.' من is the possessor ('my') and کینه‌جوی is an adjective/agentive meaning 'vengeful, seeking revenge.' The clause says the homeland 'becomes vengeful' toward the speaker.
segments.s543-g67: دریغ is an exclamatory noun meaning 'pity' or 'alas.' آن + پراگندن is a nominalized clause 'the scattering (that...)' where آن points to the following action. گنج من = 'my treasure' (noun + possessor). The whole segment expresses regret about the scattering of the speaker's treasure.
segments.s543-g68: فرستادن is a verbal noun 'the sending' or 'dispatching' (here nominal), followed by مردم 'people' (object of that sending). The conjunction و links this to رنج من 'my suffering/hardship' (رنج with the possessor من). The clause lists wasted efforts: sending men and the speaker's toil/suffering.
segments.s543-g69: ز مهرک = 'from Mehrak' (ز = 'from', مهرک the proper name). یکی دختری = 'one daughter' (یکی as numeral/indefinite + noun); ماند = 'remained/was left' (past). و بس is a final, emphatic phrase 'and that's all / only.' The clause says only one daughter of Mehrak survived.
segments.s543-g70: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that.' او را uses the object marker را with 'she/her' (here marking the person referred to). به جهرم = 'to/in Jahrom' (ز + place phrase: here = motion/state with the city). ندیدست کس is a negative existential phrasing: ندیدست = 'has not been seen / was not seen' and کس = 'anyone/no one' (in negative context meaning 'no one'). Together: 'that no one in Jahrom has seen her.'
segments.s543-g71: بفرمایم here is a first-person hortative/performative 'I will say / let me say' (poetic register); اکنون = 'now.' که introduces the content clause. جوینده باز = 'the seeker, again' where باز is the adverb 'again' modifying the seeker/returning action. The clause sets up a report about where the seeker came from.
segments.s543-g72: A simple enumerative prepositional series: ز روم = 'from Rome' (روم here = Byzantium/Europe), و ز چین = 'and from China', و ز هند = 'and from India', و طراز = 'and Taraz' (final element). The repeated ز marks 'from' for each place; طراز here is a toponym (a place name) in the list.
segments.s543-g17: بداند is a 3rd-person present (he knows) formed with the verbal stem دان and the archaic/preverbal b- marker; it governs the following noun phrase. شمار ("number") is the direct object, followed by the noun phrase سپهر بلند ("lofty/heavenly sky") which functions as the thing being counted (i.e. 'the number of the lofty heavens') even though the ezāfe/genitive marker is omitted in this poetic diction.
segments.s543-g18: در پادشاهی is a prepositional phrase ('in the realm' / 'in matters of kingship') that limits the domain of the previous clause. The conjunction و links another nominal phrase راه گزند ('the way/path of harm'), so the sentence lists two domains of his knowledge: celestial reckoning and routes/ways of danger within the kingdom.
segments.s543-g19: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). هفت کشور is a numeral + noun ('seven countries/realms'). ت + را together form the enclitic object 'ترا' ('to you' / 'yours'), with the clitic pronoun (ت) appearing before the object marker را as is common in older Persian. بی همال is an adjective phrase meaning 'without equal / peerless' that modifies 'هفت کشور'.
segments.s543-g20: بخواهد is the 3rd-person present/future of خواستن ('he wants / he will wish'); here it signals the conditional's apodosis ('if he wishes ...'). بدن is the direct object ('the body') and بازیابد is the verb 'regain / restore' (3rd-person present/future form). به فال is an instrumental prepositional phrase ('by/through augury/divination'), where به carries the 'by' sense and فال = 'omen/divination'.
segments.s543-g21: یکایک is an adverb meaning 'one by one' or 'each in turn'. بگوید here functions as a 3rd-person verb ('he says / he will say') in the narrative (the listed dictionary senses do not show the verbal sense but the word is used verbally). ندارد (negative of داشتن) means 'he has not / there is no' and with به رنج ('with hardship') yields the idiom 'ندارد به رنج' = 'it causes no trouble / it is not difficult' (literal: 'has not with hardship').
segments.s543-g22: نخواهد is the negative of خواهد ('he will not / he does not want to') and here functions to mean 'he will not demand / he will not take'. The word list splits بدین into ب + دین, but the correct reading in the sentence is بدین = 'با + این' ('for/with this' = 'for this answer'); thus بدین پاسخ = 'for this answer'. از شاه گنج is a prepositional phrase 'from the king's treasure/treasury', so the whole phrase means 'he will not take (or demand) from the royal treasury for this answer' — the verb 'take/charge' is understood from the context and is expressed by نخواهد.
segments.s543-g23: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 3rd-person singular 'he heard'. بگزید is the past 3rd-person singular of گزیدن ('he chose/selected'). In this poetic/archaic word order the verbs precede the subject (شاه اردشیر = 'the king, Ardashir' as the person chosen/focus follows the verb).
segments.s543-g24: جوانی is an appositional noun meaning 'a youth / young man' describing Ardashir. گرانمایه is an adjective 'valuable / esteemed' modifying جوانی, and the coordinating و links the following adjective تیزویر, an archaic/compound adjective meaning something like 'sharp/quick in prowess or action' — all together forming a descriptive apposition ('a noble young man and swift/keen in action').

sentences.s543-s41: The wise man proudly rose like a dark cypress, adorned with beauty and with nobility.
sentences.s543-s42: To me, in that land she had no equal; in the realm there was no such tall cypress.
sentences.s543-s17: And if this had not been — that I should be in hardship — the treasure likewise would not have been scattered in this way.
sentences.s543-s18: The king's envoy came as part of a stratagem, with gifts and offerings.
sentences.s543-s19: He said what the king had told him: all the secrets were revealed from concealment.
sentences.s543-s20: He asked him about the plot and became sorrowful; from questioning he turned toward knowledge and a remedy.
sentences.s543-s21: He brought the ṣalāb and seized a star; he took a Roman zij into his possession.
sentences.s543-s22: He fixed his gaze on the work of the lofty heavens, concerning ease and profit and suffering and harm.
sentences.s543-s23: He told the messenger the tally: I have counted (the numbers) from Iran and from the sovereign's star.
sentences.s543-s24: If from the essence of little Mehrak a newborn should arise, let this seed be mingled with that lineage.
sentences.s543-s29: If he does not turn away from this saddle, the lofty heavens will bring it about; now I said, "Go, noble one."
sentences.s543-s30: The messenger came to the king and told (him) that which he had heard from that renowned man.
sentences.s543-s31: When Ardashir heard his speech, his heart became full of sorrow and his face was like Zarir's.
sentences.s543-s32: He said to the messenger, 'May it never be that I see anyone of Mehrak's lineage.'
sentences.s543-s5: He said that he would ask the Maker of the world about both the manifest and the hidden.
sentences.s543-s6: That I may hold the world in my hand without enemies, and be nothing but joyful and God‑worshipping.
sentences.s543-s7: To him the auspicious one said his command: 'O king, bright‑hearted and path‑seeker,'
sentences.s543-s8: Send someone toward the Indian plot — someone who pursues knowledge and is a rescuer (a succorer).
sentences.s543-s1: When Shapur became like a tall cypress, he feared harm from the evil eye.
sentences.s543-s2: You were not apart from Ardashir even for a time; to him you were like a director — you were a vizier.
sentences.s543-s3: You did not keep the king away from battle for a single day; there was no need to delay in rejoicing his absence.
sentences.s543-s4: When you met the enemy in some place, again you raised the head of the evildoer.
sentences.s543-s13: He sent to the sage in India many horses, dinars, and some birds.
sentences.s543-s14: He said to him: 'Go, speak before the sage, saying "O man Nīk-akhtar and seeker of the road..."'
sentences.s543-s15: Keep watch over Akhtar, for until I am free from war and bring the country into my grasp...
sentences.s543-s16: If he wishes this arrangement, then by the counsel of that strength show the way.
sentences.s543-s37: If I find him on the fire, I will roast him; I will make the earth wretched and weeping.
sentences.s543-s38: He sent several horsemen to Jahrom; one was a certain man — a seeker and resentful.
sentences.s543-s39: When the maiden Mehrak became aware, she leapt up; she went toward the khan Mahter and sat in a corner.
sentences.s543-s40: When that maiden Mehrak sat down, she gave (something) and made him esteemed to me — she honored him greatly.
sentences.s543-s25: If he sits calmly upon the king's throne, one should not send troops in every direction.
sentences.s543-s26: He increases his treasure and lessens his hardship; you take up hatred — weigh these two gems.
sentences.s543-s27: If he does this, Iran becomes his rightfully, and he will attain every desire of the heart.
sentences.s543-s28: He gave something to the messenger and said, 'Of all that I have said, nothing should be hidden.'
sentences.s543-s33: I will take shelter inside the enemy's house, leaving my quarter; my homeland will become hostile toward me.
sentences.s543-s34: It is a pity that my treasure was scattered, and that my sending of men and my hardships (came to nothing).
sentences.s543-s35: From Mehrak only one daughter remained, and no one in Jahrom has seen her.
sentences.s543-s36: Now I will say that the seeker returned again from Rome and from China and from India and (from) Taraz.
sentences.s543-s9: He knows the number of the lofty heavens and the path of harm in the realm.
sentences.s543-s10: If he wishes to make seven peerless realms yours, he can restore the body by divination.
sentences.s543-s11: One by one he will say, 'It causes no hardship,' and he will not demand any payment from the royal treasury for this answer.
sentences.s543-s12: When the king heard, he chose Ardashir — a noble young man and swift in prowess.
